intro:
The lake of fire is the term used in Revelation for the eschatological place of ultimate and eternal judgment on the wicked. It appears five times in Revelation (19:20; 20:10, 14, 15; 21:8). Death and Hades are cast into it (20:14), identifying it as the final destination beyond the temporary realm of the dead. The beast and false prophet are thrown into it alive at Christ's return (19:20); Satan is cast into it after the millennium (20:10); and at the last judgment, all whose names are not found in the book of life are cast into it (20:15). Revelation 20:14 explicitly identifies the lake of fire as 'the second death' — a phrase appearing in Jewish apocalyptic tradition (Targums) for the eschatological death of the wicked.

The lake of fire draws on OT images of divine judgment: the burning sulfur of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen. 19:24), the 'fire and brimstone' of prophetic judgment (Ezek. 38:22; Isa. 30:33), and Gehenna — the Valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem where child sacrifice to Moloch was practiced, which became a metaphor for the place of punishment (Jer. 7:31-32; 2 Kings 23:10). Jesus's use of Gehenna (Matt. 5:22; 10:28; Mark 9:43-48) and Paul's references to 'everlasting destruction' (2 Thess. 1:9) contribute to the tradition that Revelation crystallizes in the lake of fire image. The phrase 'tormented day and night forever and ever' (Rev. 20:10) describing the devil, beast, and false prophet has been central to the debate between eternal conscious punishment and annihilationism in Christian eschatology.
